data.title: Ladder Capital: High Margin Of Dividend Safety
data.description:
Ladder Capital Corp (NYSE:LADR) is set for a robust 2025 financial year if the mortgage real estate investment trust's fourth quarter earnings are any indication of the REIT's performance potential.

Ladder Capital managed to out-earn its dividend with distributable profits in 4Q24 and even has potential to grow its dividend pay-out in 2025.

While Ladder Capital has managed to avoid a dividend cut amid robust loan performance in 2024, other mortgage real estate investment trusts, like Blackstone Mortgage Trust, Inc. (BXMT), were not that lucky.

Ladder Capital right-sized its portfolio and still offers passive income investors a high margin of safety as far as the dividend is concerned.

I think that Ladder Capital is still one of the best mortgage REITs, focused on loan origination in the CRE market, that passive income investors can buy, and I predict that shareholders will get a raise in 2025.

Improving Provisioning Situation And Payout Health Indicate Potential For Dividend Growth In 2025

Ladder Capital is a mortgage real estate investment trust whose core expertise is the origination of Senior Secured Loans in the commercial real estate market. That said, the trust also owns commercial real estate securities as well as actual commercial real estate investments.

As of December 31, 2024, the mortgage trust owned a portfolio comprised of real estate investments worth $4.9 billion (including restricted cash), down about $700 million since the end of 2023.

The commercial real estate market has seen considerable stress in the last two years as higher short-term interest rates weighed on investors' ability to refinance short-duration commercial real estate loans and the pandemic catalyzed changes in the work culture, with more people working remotely or entering some kind of hybrid work arrangements. The latter trend has pushed up vacancy rates for office properties, as of December 31, 2024, to 20% on a national level, which in turn caused a higher number of office loan defaults.

One of the most famous example of the distress in the office market was represented by private equity company Blackstone Inc. (BX) walking away from a 621,000-square-foot office tower at 1740 Broadway in Manhattan in 2022.

In a market that has been characterized by higher loan defaults and deteriorating investment fundamentals, Ladder Capital has so far avoided a dividend cut and still posts very healthy dividend pay-out metrics, thanks to a well-performing loan portfolio.

The same could not be said about Blackstone Mortgage Trust, for instance, which was forced to cut down its dividend by 24% in 2024 amid a rise in problematic office loans.

The good news for Ladder Capital is that the mortgage trust did not have to add to its loan loss reserves in the fourth quarter, suggesting healthy loan quality. In 4Q24, Ladder Capital's decreased four quarters in a row and finally dropped to zero.

As opposed to Blackstone Mortgage Trust, Ladder Capital's 4Q24 dividend coverage was also excellent, providing further support to the idea that Ladder Capital might be a superior investment choice for passive income investors.

Ladder Capital earned $0.27 per share in distributable earnings in the last quarter, which compares to an unchanged dividend pay-out of $0.23 per share. This in turn translates into a dividend Payout Ratio of 85% in 4Q24.

Since Ladder Capital's dividend Payout Ratio averaged just 76% in the last year, I assume that the mortgage real estate investment might want to raise its dividend in 2025.

Despite cutting its dividend down to $0.48 per share per quarter last year, Blackstone Mortgage Trust did not cover its dividend with distributable profits in 4Q24. BXMT's dividend Payout Ratio in the fourth quarter was 107% and the trust under-earned its dividend by $0.03 per share due to persistent loan losses in the company's office loan portfolio.

Discount To BV Not Deserved

Taking into account Ladder Capital's pay-out health, which is supported by a strong loan performance and improving provisions, the mortgage real estate investment trust should not be selling at a discount to book value. This is particularly true, in my view, because the mortgage trust is growing its book value as well. At the very least, I anticipate Ladder Capital to re-rate to book value which as of December 31, 2024, stood at $12.08 per share, up eight cents per share QoQ.

At the time of writing, Ladder Capital is selling for a 4% discount to book value, whereas Blackstone Mortgage Trust is selling at a bigger 8% discount. However, it should be said here, that Blackstone Mortgage Trust's dividend cut improved the trust's dividend pay-out metrics in 3Q24 which restored some investor confidence.

Starwood Property Trust, Inc. (STWD), which I would hold up as the gold standard in the commercial real estate industry taking into account the trust's long track record and stable $0.48 per share per quarter dividend, is selling for 1.02x book value.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Disappoint

Mortgage real estate investment trusts hand out floating-rate loans and thus obviously benefit from higher interest rates. In the case that we were to see a drastic decline in inflation and the central bank were to lower short-term interest rates, Ladder Capital may see pressure building on its net interest income.

Loan losses, particularly with regards to the trust's office loan portfolio, are a potential headwind for mortgage REIT and its investors as well.

My Conclusion

Ladder Capital is still very much a very solid investment choice for passive income investors that want to add a top-performing trust to their portfolios.

Ladder Capital's credit provisioning situation is brightening up and the mortgage real estate investment trust is seeing healthy pay-out metrics, which cannot be said about its peer Blackstone Mortgage Trust.

The discount to book value, as far as I am concerned, is not really justified given the healthy performance profile of Ladder Capital in the fourth quarter.

In my view, Ladder Capital as well as Starwood Property Trust remain highly compelling long-term buy-and-hold investments, and I would not be surprised to see Ladder Capital raise its dividend in 2025.

data.title: STEW: Another Distribution Bump For This Year
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

SRH Total Return Fund (NYSE:STEW) is an older closed-end fund that provides significant exposure to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B). This is considered a "non-diversified" fund due to this concentration. It's often considered one way to get a deeply discounted exposure to BRK, but also paying a quarterly distribution. Heading into 2025, they once again lifted the payout, now the fourth year in a row of bumping the distribution.

Since our last update, around a year ago, the fund has put up respectable total returns. Though they slightly underperformed against the S&P 500 Index. Some of this slight underperformance can be attributed to the discount widening out a bit further during this period. However, it is worth noting that over the longer term, STEW has underperformed this broader index.

STEW Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.26
  • Discount: 22%
  • Distribution Yield: 4.01%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.16%
  • Leverage: 9.83%
  • Managed Assets: $2.273 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

STEW's investment objective is "total return." To achieve this objective, the fund "utilizes a bottom-up, value-driven investment process to identify securities of good quality businesses trading below estimated intrinsic value."

Concentrated Exposure And Leverage

As they note in their own material, the Berkshire exposure is well laid out to investors and noted:

The Board feels it is important that stockholders be aware of the Fund’s high concentration in a small number of positions. Concentrating investments in fewer securities may involve a degree of risk that is greater than a fund having less concentrated investments spread over a greater number of securities. In particular, the Fund is highly concentrated in Berkshire Hathaway, Inc., which, in addition to other business risks, is largely dependent on Warren Buffett for major investment and capital allocation decisions. When Mr. Buffett is no longer able to fulfill his responsibilities with Berkshire Hathaway, Inc., the value of the Fund’s position in Berkshire Hathaway, Inc. could be materially impacted.

At this time, both the class A and B shares account for 38% of the fund. While it is important to note that STEW is non-diversified because of this concentration, BRK itself is a diversified conglomerate. Given the several business operations under their roof and investment portfolio, some investors could consider that diversified enough anyway.

With STEW, investors are also exposed to a bit of leverage through borrowings. Any leverage will add additional risks due to greater volatility. That said, this fund is fairly lightly leveraged, and they are based on low fixed rates of interest. At this time, most funds are paying 5% or more for their borrowings, while STEW is still enjoying sub-3% rates.

When including the leverage expenses, the fund's total expense ratio comes to 1.49%. That's where an investor can argue that while STEW is a discounted way to gain exposure to BRK in a meaningful way, it also comes with a higher cost than buying BRK shares outright.

Performance - Deep And Persistent Discount

As we touched on above, the fund has seen underperformance relative to the S&P 500 Index, which can be represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). What may be interesting, though, is that a more appropriate measuring stick could be the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP). RSP could be more appropriate as SPY has become significantly concentrated in the information technology sector and is more representative of the Magnificent 7 names these days compared to being a real broad market measurement.

When comparing STEW to RSP, we see that the fund's performance on a total NAV return basis is nearly identical over the last decade. On a total return basis, STEW has slightly outperformed.

Another comparison worth making here is putting STEW up against BRK itself. We also see that they had underperformed, but that didn't start to happen meaningfully until around 2022.

The contributing factors for this would be having an expense ratio and a slightly varied portfolio. While the fund's largest exposure by far is to BRK A and B, they still have another ~60% over the years allocated to other various holdings.

However, one of the main arguments for owning STEW would be the fund's massive discount. This has been a deep and persistent discount for a number of years, and it has actually widened out even further over the last few years now.

A Perfect Activist Target? Not Really

If you believe this would be a perfect target for an activist group such as Saba to come in and shake things up, that would be nearly impossible. The underlying portfolio is concentrated and similarly, the ownership of the fund is concentrated as well.

On November 30, 2024, trusts and other entities and individuals affiliated with Stewart R. Horejsi and the Horejsi family owned 45,384,254 shares of Common Stock of the Fund, representing 47.06% of the total Common Stock outstanding.

Stewart Horejsi was previously the manager of the fund, but in 2022, the fund saw new managers, a new name and a new ticker. Given the concentration in ownership, though, there likely isn't going to be a significant change in the fund at this time.

That doesn't mean that there haven't been any efforts in trying to reduce the discount. The fund has repurchased shares, but these are relatively small amounts given the size of the fund.

I also believe that increasing the distribution regularly is another effort to potentially reduce the discount. Most CEF investors just want really high yields, and the actual distribution rate here isn't that attractive. However, thanks to such a large discount, the fund's NAV rate comes to 3.10% while the rate on the share price comes closer to 4%.

Further, if they keep up this pattern of raising annually going forward, it could start to attract some investors. Dividend growth-oriented investors could start to take notice. That said, historically speaking, consistency in a distribution policy hasn't been a focus.

A normal question from here would be how they fund the distribution in the first place because their most significant exposure doesn't pay a dividend at all. That's where it goes back to being in more familiar territory with an equity-focused CEF, which is to say that even with such a low NAV rate, the fund primarily covers its payouts through capital gains (i.e., shares of BRK and other underlying portfolio positions need to keep rising so they can trim the profits and pay those out to investors.)

Conclusion

STEW is unlikely to be appealing to most CEF investors, with its relatively low distribution rate and concentration. However, it could be appropriate for some others. The massive discount is certainly one of the appealing factors, as it has widened out over the last couple of years. That said, an activist group can't really come in and exert enough pressure due to concentrated ownership of the previous manager's family. Still, they have been a bit more consistent in recent years with increasing the distribution. It may not be at a high rate, but it could attract some dividend growth investors if they continue to remain consistent.

data.title: Rates Spark: Europe Bracing To Spend On Defense
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker & Benjamin Schroeder

Spending needs clear as EU security landscape rapidly evolves

The US pushing ahead and engaging with Russia over a deal in Ukraine has instilled a sense of urgency among European leaders. Events are evolving fast, with US and Russian officials meeting in Saudi Arabia today to discuss ending the war in Ukraine - with neither Europe nor Ukraine having a seat at the table.

At least at the start of the week, the rates market's focus appeared to be on the supply implications of Europe's drastically changing security landscape, as EU officials signalled the need for an immediate ramping up of military spending and an easing of fiscal rules to accommodate the added funding need.

The prospect of joint borrowing to fund the EU's defence needs had also received more tailwinds, as the frontrunner of the upcoming German elections indicated some openness to the idea. It may still take months, with Friedrich Merz of the German CDU pointing to upcoming discussions at the NATO summit in The Hague in June. The outcome of the German elections on 23 February are part of the puzzle. The composition of parliament will determine how easy it will be for Germany to reform its debt brake, which needs a 2/3 majority.

Long end Bund yields rose by up to 6bp, also partially unwinding the outperformance versus swaps that was witnessed at the end of last week. The 30Y Bund yields at 2.74% is still some 10bp off from January's peaks when European Central Bank (ECB) pricing was at its most hawkish point. For now, the front end reaction has been more muted, with the terminal rate pricing for the ECB still anchored at around 2% to slightly lower.

Final proposal for change in Dutch pension reforms has been published

The New Social Contract (NSC) coalition party in the Netherlands published a final version of its legal proposal last week, which could potentially disrupt the ongoing Dutch pension fund reforms. The latest adjustments to the proposal would allow pension funds to avoid a vote of approval by offering an opt-out to each participant instead. Those that object would only see their new contributions move to the reformed defined contributions system; existing assets would remain under the defined benefits scheme. For pension funds, the downside of choosing the opt-out path would be the need to run two pension pots in parallel, while a successful vote would allow a complete transition of assets to the new system. Parliament will discuss the proposed amendment in early March.

The potential impact from this additional feature remains difficult to assess, as the outcome would rely on the choices made by both pension funds and their participants. Assuming the legal amendment makes it through both parliament and the senate (which still seems unlikely), then pension funds would have to decide which path to take. Surveys suggest that finding a majority to vote in favour of the new defined contributions system will prove difficult. If, for that reason, many funds decide to offer an opt-out option, then we could see a more fragmented pool of assets. Given the uncertainty, we can imagine that pension funds will take a more passive approach to rebalancing their interest rate hedges for the moment. As such, any steepening pressure due to changes in longer-dated fixed receiver demand may ease for now.

Tuesday's events and market view

US markets will be back from their holiday yesterday. With German politics in the spotlight, the outcome from the ZEW survey expectations will be closely watched. Consensus sees the expectations component improving moderately from 10 to 20. From the US, we have the empire manufacturing index and TIC data. Overall, we expect news headlines to grab more attention than data releases.
